title: The Deutsch Files IV

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: I can only start with what understanding I want. Right. And I know I've asked you this before, but I want to be pedantically exhaustive about connecting the four theories of the fabric of reality. And the reason I bring that up is because I think most people still view what you've written as being four separate things. And it's hard enough to grasp these four separate things because they're actually fairly deep and wide-ranging theories. But I think in your mind they connect together into one thing. Knowledge is a crystal and nature has no boundaries, right? These are just phrases, but these things all connect together. So we've talked in the past, for example, how epistemology and evolution connect in that they're both forms of knowledge creation. We've talked about quantum physics and computation connect to create quantum computation. I'd just love to get as many examples, how does physics connect to evolution? How does evolution connect to computation, for example? Things that may be less obvious where people might view things as different theories, but to you, they're fundamentally the same.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, evolution and epistemology, people find both of those, the connection between both of those and physics, very counterintuitive, because most people think of physics in a very bottom-up way. And I think for completely independent reasons, such as constructor theory, that's a mistake. Ever since that idea caught on, like sometime after Newton, physicists have tried to shoehorn other physical theories into that mold. And that gives rise to, for example, the problem of foundations of thermodynamics and statistical mechanics. How can you have an exact second law when the fundamental theories of physics are all time reversible and the second law is time irreversible? How can you have that? The prevailing view is yeah, thermodynamics, epistemology are both emergent theories and therefore not fundamental from the physics point of view. And therefore if we want to understand the universe at a fundamental level, we needn't bother with those. Those are just like the theory of washing machines or gardening. I think that's artificial, and especially when they have to get very embarrassed when they exclude thermodynamics from physics in that way. I think that a theory which is going to go deeper than the current paradigm of physics is going to have to put emergent phenomena and emergent theories on the same level as microscopic theories. People talk about reductionism and holism and some people are reductionists and some people are holists. I think I want to put them both in a sack and tie it up and let them come out with a resolution. There cannot be a criterion for excluding a set of theories from the body of knowledge

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And what about good explanations?

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Abandon them if they're not good explanations, but if they are, why make a class distinction between them? It's just going to lead to error, and I think it has led to error in thinking about the world. So that's the connection between physics and those two.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: What you just said, for example, is that reductionist theories aren't the only theories there are because they don't form good explanations at the level where you need them, because especially when you have emergence, then you have very unpredictable things. You're not going to calculate all the particle collisions from the Big Bang till now to figure out how humans evolved, or how species evolve. At every level of emergence, there is a possibility for an explanation that explains that level, and you need that explanation.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So I think that's very helpful. And in your thermodynamics example, like if you're trying to figure out how a steam engine works, you're not going to do statistical mechanics and trace every collision. You're going to actually probably start at thermodynamics. And so that's a tie between, I guess, is it evolution and physics? No, epistemology and physics much more.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So thermodynamics is emergent physics, and it is in terms of those four, it is epistemology. But that just shows that the terminology's misleading. The terminology in particular of the word fundamental, which I think you use slightly differently to the majority of other especially physicists.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: They hear it, and anyone listening to this hears fundamental and thinks reductionism automatically. But for example, you would say that people are a fundamental feature of reality. So what does fundamental therefore mean in this worldview?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So fundamental ideas are the ones that are needed in more types of explanations. We'll never get to the ultimate fundamental theory, because that would mean that there were no more problems. And that can't happen. But yes, so that's how I use the word fundamental.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: I think you also said something related, which is that if it reality is what is needed in your explanation, or what has to exist in the explanation for the explanation to work.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes, a rule for deciding that something is real. It's not the meaning of real, because there's things we don't understand about real, like in what sense are numbers real and abstract objects real? And in case physicists don't like that, I always ask: in what sense are laws of physics real? Because we can't trip over one, but on the other hand, we can't understand anything in physics without them. So they're real.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Some would claim that we are cognitively incapable of understanding physics full stop up to a certain point. This is an idea that Richard Dawkins has of middle world, which was cropping up very recently in discussions. Our brain evolved on the African savanna in order to survive, so it should be no mystery to us.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: agreement about what quantum theory means because in fact we are cognitively closed to understanding things that are too small, things that are outside middle world because they travel too fast or are too large like the entire cosmos.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: But this is flawed because of universality.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Among other things. We understand quasars and subatomic particles. So, if that argument had been valid, then we would have stopped a long time ago in understanding things.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Especially like mathematics, but also physics.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: And that then brings up this connection or draws out this connection between quantum physics and epistemology. Can you draw that line, that connection?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So, the nicest one I think, this is the one that Naval also likes best: the idea of knowledge as information which once it's there keeps itself in existence and gets that property by error correction. Then since there are many errors for every possible truth, many possible errors for every truth, then in the multiverse, if there's error correction, then there're a bunch of unlike universes which become alike in the region in which they're correcting errors. And so that's a striking connection between epistemology and physics.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: I hope that this kind of connection will be amplified once we get to formulating theories of knowledge and so on in constructor theory.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Constructor theory isn't limited to the parochial properties of quantum theory. It emerged from quantum theory, it emerged from problems in quantum theory, but it's a level of description—and I'm gesticulating as if it was a higher level—but actually, it's a lower level of description than quantum theory. It's more reductionist...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: ...than quantum theory and yet it is more compatible with emergent properties. We're hoping eventually that things like knowledge and economics, ultimately consciousness maybe, though I—my own view is that consciousness will require an extra idea, not just elaborating ideas from within constructor theory, it requires an idea, another idea, at least one.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Hitherto the line from Newton through to Einstein and then to quantum theory has been ever more difficult and elaborate mathematics, and so this then string theory comes along and this I think leads to the general opinion that the next theory of physics will be more mathematically challenging and hence all the reasons earlier that maybe we won't be able to understand it, but constructor theory seems to have echoes of that famous Feynman...

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: ...line where he said that perhaps the ultimate laws of physics or whatever he meant by that would be more like the rules of the checkerboard than some fancy dynamical mathematical laws. Is that what you have in mind with constructor theory, some checkerboard type notion of possible and impossible moves?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and it could be just like that. You can't understand if you have a listing of all the moves in a game of chess, you can't understand what's happening there unless you know the rules of chess. But knowing the rules of chess—the people who make chess programs think that if you know the rules of chess, you can understand all the games, but it's not true. If you want to understand games as opposed to just win them, then you need a lot more than just the rules. And so, yeah, constructor theory is just like that, we hope.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: But it's more than just physics as you say. This is an explanation that's not merely about fundamental laws of physics but is extendable into biology and—

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, or you could say that physics is going to consume all those fields, just like it's consumed many other fields. We think of constructor theory as a theory of physics. And if it consumes those fields, then that's what physics has always done.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Okay, can I back up for a second on constructor theory? Because I think it's the part that might be understood the least by the general audience. I know that Chiara wrote a book on it. You have not yet written extensively for the laypeople on it. What problem are you trying to solve, and where are you at so far, and what level of confidence do you have that this is the right approach to go down?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Huh, those are very long questions.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: It's okay, I think the answers are illuminating.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So when I wrote my first paper on constructor theory, which was a philosophical paper, didn't have really any physics, it was published in a philosophical journal, and I wanted to explain what we were trying to do, what this research program is, because we didn't know at the time—and we still don't—what the definitive axioms of constructor theory are going to be. We're going to find those as we find the applications. So I thought I would give some motivation. We don't have a good theory of initial conditions. If we think carefully, why should we need initial conditions when we are perfectly fine to have no theory of the final conditions? So that's one thing. And then the thermodynamics thing. And then there's the fact that things like computation and information can't be expressed in the initial conditions plus laws of motion way. And I thought I would write a quick paper, and I ended up with 18 motivations. I can't think now—and you can look up the paper if you want to see all 18—I can't think what they all were now, but they—this seemed to me a motivation for thinking that this constructor theory might be a more fundamental way of doing physics.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: The approach was a very simple change in world view in a certain way. You can say very quickly what the change is, but the ramifications are huge and it seems to touch all these problems in different fields.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And that's what motivated me to try to make a theory out of this.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And the next thing that happened was that I gave a talk at the Clarendon Laboratory and Chiara was in the audience and she was graduate student working on quantum information.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And she came up to me afterwards and said, 'What about so and so, that can't be right, can it? Because...' and I had indeed contradicted myself in the talk.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And so then she came round and we chatted about it and I said, 'Do you want to work on it?' because there's a lot more to work on than one person can do, especially me.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: [chuckle]

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So she said yes. And since then we've... so she's worked on the constructor theory of life, constructor theory of probability,

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: which in a way translating existing theories into constructor theoretic form and uncovering little nuggets.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: My favorite nugget is that in constructor theoretic thermodynamics, the first law is... is to do with information, not just the second law.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So the way she defines the first law, it's... it's similar to statistical mechanics, but you end up with the first law that is about 'you can do this and not that, you can do this and not that' and as a result you get the version of the first law,

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: but better than normal because it incidentally has the property that... let me see if I get this right... I think it incidentally has the property that there must be a lower bound on the energy, whereas existing thermodynamics doesn't, you have to put that in as an... as an extra axiom.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And if there were no lower bound, then you could have an object that you were just extracting energy out of without violating the second law, just first law extracting energy forever. And that would make a perpetual motion machine of the first kind.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But the existing thermodynamics you have to impose that as an extra condition and in constructor theory you don't have to. Meanwhile, I've been... we together been working on the constructor theory of time.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Perhaps I could say something about the constructor theory of time. Really the first people to have this idea were Wheeler and DeWitt, who trying to find a viable theory of quantum gravity, and they found a theory of quantum gravity in which there's no time.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Time doesn't appear in the theory. There's just different spaces, three-dimensional spaces, which all have amplitudes, quantum mechanical amplitudes, and they all exist simultaneously, and time is an emergent property of...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: of the way they are entangled with each other. But nothing ever changes. It's just an emergent property of how they're entangled. And then as Julian Barbour made a big thing out of this and wrote a whole book about it, then Page and Wootters—so that was a theory tied to general relativity. It would only work in the context of general relativity. Page and Wootters made a construction that will work in any quantum theory, not just quantum theory of gravity, but any quantum theory you can formulate a theory of time that doesn't have things changing in it and so that a static universe still has time because of entanglement.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So that is more general. It—it doesn't require Wheeler and DeWitt's specific theory of—of spacetime. It's—and when I saw Page and Wootters' theory, I immediately thought, yeah, this is it, this is what time is. And then many people have tried to get something more out of it, and they failed. And then Sam came along and got something more out of it, so he's got the most advanced theory of—of time in—in the universe, in the multiverse, or whatever, so far. In a way, it's only a small improvement on Page and Wootters, but it is more general, and that's what we want. And then Chiara and I are working on the constructor theory of time, which we hope will be the sort of for the moment the ultimate version of that, which instead of deriving the concepts from some theory like quantum theory, it lays down rules that it says any theory that obeys these rules will have time. And Chiara already did that with probability. So it says any theory that obeys these rules will have probability, even though there's no stochastic processes. So—it has probability in the sense that quantum theory does, that—that an observer, a rational observer, will bet in certain ways, but nothing can happen in—in more—everything's deterministic. Constructor theory, by the way, is necessarily deterministic. It—it can't work unless everything's deterministic.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: But your view of the quantum multiverse is already deterministic.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes. Yes. So quantum multiverse obeys that rule. And I've—I've also written that the—the prevailing concept of a stochastic theory that just doesn't make sense. It's—it's like saying—saying that something's more probable is like saying it has more magic. It—it's—nothing follows from that statement. You need an extra theory to make sense of that statement. Yeah, and now I'm working on a universal constructor.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Is universal constructor related a little bit to your—talk in Fabric of Reality about—virtual reality generators?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Only—so that's an interesting thought.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: I don't think it's related to except in the general way that the fact that there is such a thing as virtual reality, that you can make a virtual reality machine in our universe and that it can simulate anything, then that's Turing universality or computational universality. That's in constructor theory, you hardly have to say that. Like it's so built-in that it is the most natural thing, and you'd have to work very hard and get over some very difficult obstacles if that weren't true in constructor theory.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So far in constructor theory, you've basically got some better or more universal explanations of existing theories. When do we start getting predictions? When do we start getting tests?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So, I guess having a lower bound for the energy is a prediction. Not a very strong prediction because everyone believed that already. I think that where constructor theory will start making new... where people will start formulating theories about new things within the constructor theory framework... that will be when these new things combine existing theories where it was unexpected before. Having... now I don't believe in the quantum theory of life, for example. I think that's a dead end, a blind alley. But that sort of thing is the sort of thing that constructor theory could provide a framework for that existing theories don't have a framework for. It's a pity. It's like, I thought in The Fabric of Reality, I thought that the multiverse kind of solved the problem of free will. I didn't quite say that there, but I hinted that maybe it solves the problem of free will, and I don't think it does at all. But even though it doesn't solve it, it gives a framework in which one can think, because free will involves counterfactuals. Counterfactuals have much more meaning in the theory of the multiverse than they do in a Newtonian or classical theory. And in constructor theory, they have even more meaning. That's the kind of thing. I can't foretell the growth of knowledge. I don't even know that constructor theory is going to work. It could end up as a kind of curiosity that this is what people in the early 21st century... some people thought would illuminate things, but it only provides a little bit of illumination and now we know what the real illumination is, we don't need it. That might be, or it might be the opposite.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: This is a meta question. I don't want to take us off on a tangent, but you just said there, as a good Popperian would, that constructor theory could be completely wrong and it will just be a footnote in the history of physics. There are other people out there with new theories as well. They do not speak in a Popperian way and they will say about their new theory that they're holding up that this is going to solve the origin of life...

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: This is going to tell us what the nature of time is, this is going to solve what the pre-conditions of the Big Bang were and so on with great confidence. And they, I have to say, get a lot more attention, and they get the investment, and they appear on some big podcasts and things. Is this an inherent disadvantage of being a Popperian that we couch our terms in such conjectural language?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: I suppose if you were fanatical about getting approval and funding and then you would maximize that, but I mean I think people working on things, usually the people working on things, deep things for their own sake, for the sake of those things, are the ones that tend to make progress. In some cases that will make them famous like if you invent M-RNA vaccines then it will make you famous though those people weren't famous before. And I think I'm always suspicious when someone says what they're going to invent or what they're going to prove or what they're going to find. I would rather they said this is a problem I'm going to address. I don't know where it's going. And here's my problem, you know, we find it interesting. We think it might do so and so, but maybe it won't, maybe it'll do something we haven't thought of, you know? I think that's, I find that more attractive as a stance, like when I'm thinking of someone else's elevator pitch. I think Popper used to say what is your problem? What are you working on? And in research, when you meet someone who is not in your field, the first thing you say is 'What are you working on?', you know? That's not 'What are you going to find?', that would be a weird question.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: This is a little historical and I know you don't necessarily like to go into your own thought process because we're not here to copy you, but I think it is illuminating to think about the connection you made between physics and computation. You basically have created the or helped create the quantum theory of computation. And there are a lot of researchers and technologists now working on quantum computers, tons of them. In fact, we just talked to Maria, she's off to work at some quantum computing company.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Nice.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: And not all of these people believe in the quantum theory of the multiverse. Did you start with Hugh Everett's many worlds theory and then get to quantum computation or was that and do you view that as fundamental, like can you explain quantum computation with the observer collapse theory or is that just contradictory?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes, so as you may know, when I first came up with the idea of quantum computer, I didn't even think of it as a quantum computer. I didn't call it that. What I was trying to do is, so the consensus at the time among people who work on Everett's multiverse theory was that the multiverse theory gives the same experimental predictions as collapse theory. And in a way that was already known

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: to be false, that's the paradox of Wigner's friend and you know how

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: people just take paradoxes as 'Oh yeah, that's a paradox.' They don't think that's a reason why we're wrong, fundamentally wrong in our worldview.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Mm-hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So I thought, I thought that Everett's theory must be testable. And it was testable because it proposed a different dynamics from collapse, all the wave function collapse theories.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So I thought, okay, how would we test it? And I thought simplest thing: you do a version of the two-slit experiment,

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: then you have to have a measurement of that measurement. And how do you do that? I thought, 'Oh, it could be done with a computer.' So there's this computer that

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: but if the computer is going to have quantum... the two-slit experiment or whatever you call it, the Stern-Gerlach experiment or whatever they're all equivalent really. If it's going to have that in it, then the computer can't be decoherent. So it can't be a classical computer. So it must be augmented with these quantum operations.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Which operations would you need? So I added some operations to make a coherent classical computer with these extra operations. And that's what it was in my mind. It was just an object, it was an object connected to a computer with certain sets of... What I was interested in is something else: it was this experiment that you could do.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So then I wrote a paper about that, which among other things... it was only one section of the paper that had that in it. And then that paper was rejected and the story that they said 'that's philosophy' and I was like 'Okay, I don't care.'

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So I put it aside and I didn't... I didn't get it out again. I would tell people about it, like when I was talking to other physicists. I told Roger Penrose and I said 'Okay, now you've got to... you've got to accept that if quantum theory is true, universally true, then Everett is right.'

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And he said 'Yeah, but quantum theory isn't true.' Okay, that's a consistent position, but that's not the position that the vast majority of physicists take.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So then at the same conference where I asked Roger Penrose that, I was also talking to someone who worked for a publisher and he said 'Why don't you submit it?' and I was like 'Oh, it's too much trouble' and eventually I submitted it and it was eventually published at the same time as my

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: universal quantum computers paper, even though it came much earlier. And people think that I thought of them in the opposite order. But I didn't. I first thought of the experiment to test Everett and then much later thought about quantum computers.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Is this the same experiment that's mentioned in the Beginning of Infinity as going inside the mind of the AI?

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: I see. So you came up with that first to, so you had to create the experiment to test Many Worlds.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: and said okay, is the observer making a difference? Let's get inside the observer.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: But if we get inside the observer,

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: it's the double slit experiment all over again unless there's a quant- different kind of computer than the classical computer, it's not going to be affected by the same interference effects that the double slit experiment is vulnerable to, so we need to create qubits or quantum computing, what, what...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, again, the term qubit wasn't invented until much, much later and I- as I said, I didn't think of it like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So you were solving a different problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Yeah, a different problem.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: and it didn't cross my mind that this was a new mode of computation.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: And to do this test we'll need AGI as well.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's not my fault.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: It's the opposition that wants the conscious observer to do something different. Okay, in that case, you put a conscious observer in.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: It needs AGI and it needs quantum computing. It needs both.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: The AGI has to be a quantum computer internally.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: It has to be running on a quantum computer.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Because the alternative perspective is, otherwise you have interference.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Well, yeah, it's the opposition that is saying that it is the observer collapsing the wave function, and so if that's their position, then we have to have an observer that can observe things without collapsing wave functions.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but this observer doesn't do much that's quantum. He only- he only does a few quantum operations like half a dozen quantum operations, but most of the time he's thinking about things like 'do I know this' and so on. And that's all done with classical operations, but done with coherent quantum objects, so they have to be done with qubits instead of bits. Yeah, so in relation to what we were saying before, I would never have said at the time, 'I'm going to- I'm going to invent quantum computers.' It didn't occur to me. Even when I finally did, it was- I wasn't even trying to do that. I was- like I had this conversation with Charlie Bennett

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: where he said all complexity, I said complexity is arbitrary because it depends on the hardware. And he said, but the hardware is physics.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So I said, okay, if the hardware is physics, then you're using the wrong physics. So I thought I would go home and recast Turing's analysis in quantum physics instead of with paper tape like he did. And then I saw that there were algorithms that didn't have classical analogs.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: We talked earlier about how knowledge is that which replicates itself in the environment or get- tends to get replicated in the environment, not necessarily replicates itself. Yeah. Although it does in evolution. But, uh, tends to get replicated in the environment. So if you were able to peek across the multiverse, you would see that error correction leads to the same or similar knowledge across the multiverse. Is that true within a given universe as well? Because universe is fairly large. There should be error correction processes. Maybe they're going on elsewhere.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: We know that there's such a thing as...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Convergent evolution. full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch

But the thing we should expect, when we first encounter... the thing I expected when I first realized that there were going to be anti-rational memes, is that they were going to get more and more anti-rational. The fact that they're going to eventually kill the subculture that has them doesn't stop them, any more than the peacock's tail sooner or later will result in the extinction of the peacock species. full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch

full_transcript.text: I don't know what to do about freeing the schools and universities because there isn't like a cadre of what I call liberal genuinely liberal teachers who who could go in to replace them. full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: In a way they're the most stuck and frozen institutions. I got a tour of the Oxford colleges recently, some of them, and it was all about tradition. This is exactly the way it was 300 years ago, this is exactly the way it was 150 years ago, and uh it was interesting to see a mix of such tradition along with you know it's supposed to be so much new creativity in education.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: They've kept exactly the wrong things and they've changed things to exactly the wrong things. So they still have enacting rituals, but enacting rituals isn't our culture. And on the other hand they've got all sorts of subjects that didn't exist then because they're useless and existing subject useful subjects are being taken over.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Richard Dawkins almost got cancelled previous time...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Richard Dawkins almost got canceled previous time when he went to New Zealand and they said that they're incorporating traditional modes of understanding physics and he was like no there is only one mode of understanding physics and they were like that's racist.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: We already have that in Australia. The syllabus is replete with indigenous modes of doing science.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Well this was a few years ago so maybe it's done in Australia in New Zealand and Australia. This is terrible and it's going to cause everything to deteriorate. I don't think it will cause the destruction of our culture but once our culture has deteriorated enough then the Chinese can walk in.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: I look forward to the indigenous electric motor.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: There was a that's a famous case. There was a book written by this what was his name uh Dark Emu's the name of the book where it's just a revisionist history that claims that prior to European settlement in Australia the indigenous people were farming they had agriculture they were on their way to factories and all this.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: ...stuff and people just lap it up. And it's all — it is making its way into the curriculum now as well. Bruce Pascoe's his name, yeah, he claims to be indigenous but he's not.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: That goes to our capacity to error correct. Misinformation is a fashionable term, but it's also labels are a very genuine phenomenon. There was, I think it was only a few months ago that Osama bin Laden's letter to America or whatever was going around being praised by certain college students who thought yeah, this, not understanding that this was not a good guy.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Okay, so maybe the way to go if we can't restore 1950s schools, how about restoring 1950s curricula? That's something that the government could do. The national curriculum of course is an abomination. But maybe it's one of those things that can be, as a transitional thing, have the national curriculum now, and it's full of woke stuff. The gov— it's in the government's power to change that, to make it into true stuff. The government could change the national curriculum, and then it may be that this woke stuff, I said it's a fad, it's not — how can I put this — it's not an evolved thing. It's not a blind alley that culture has gone down. It's a fad. So it could be that after a generation of teaching old-fashioned history, history teachers will think 'that's what it is', rather than... because it's the teachers and the pupils are getting the revisionist history at the moment. If they got another history, would they even notice? Maybe they wouldn't even notice. I don't know, maybe that's too optimistic. But I get the feeling that it's all shallow. If I think of lefties of the 20s and 30s, they believed stuff. They believed in the capitalist class stealing the fruits of the labor of the working class and that this is why the working class are poor and all that stuff and the remedy is — so they had a theory. Whereas I don't think woke is a theory.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: It's a denial of a whole bunch of otherwise good pieces of knowledge, whether it be science or enlightenment ideas.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: But as I've often said, the negation of an explanation is not an explanation. So they don't have a rival worldview except the worldview that you're supposed to say certain things, not say other things and so on. But it's not — it's not a worldview in the sense that communism was.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: I think it might be an evolution of communism, of — its because there are a lot of communist ideas at the core of it. And they tend to end up on the same side of the communists — a lot of outcomes.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but do they have a theory that anything about factories...

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: The factories. They do have a theory that there're people keeping them down, that for example inflation is up because of price gouging. That's their current theory.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So that's the theory that those left-wing people used to have. They used to have it because it came from their explanation of the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: The finite resource explanation of the world.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And more complicated things as well. But I think if you ask a woke demonstrator, maybe I'm wrong, but maybe you could try it. Ask them how, how is inflation caused by the greed of the factory owners or whatever it is. Now a communist in 1950 could have told you, but I don't think a woke person today can tell you.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: Like when they ask from the river, they're chanting from the river to the sea, and somebody asks them which river and which sea, and they don't know.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: (Laughs)

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: I mean, but to your earlier point, these anti-rational memes don't have to be correct. They can drive species extinction, and the next species can figure out what was true and what wasn't.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I don't think that's what they... I don't think they're poison. I think they are a burden.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: They could be poisonous in the sense that they drive certain voting blocks, and then those voting blocks drive policies of nations. So I think your home land is facing exactly this issue, which is now I think 20, 30 percent of the Muslims are in the West and they're voting. And so the traditional support for Israel is disappearing.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: There are plenty of Muslims who are pro-Israel. The mindless ones are anti-Israel. Like I just tweeted today that, what's he called? Travel in Israel video blog was talking about the collaboration of the Palestinians with the Nazis during World War II. And he happened to mention, I think he should have made much more of this, that the Grand Mufti was going around Europe recruiting Muslims to the Nazi cause. In Albania, he totally failed. In Albania, the absolute opposite. They were one of two countries that rescued almost all their Jews. And one was Denmark, and they didn't have that many, it was only about 6,000 in the whole of Denmark, but they rescued almost all of them. But the Albanians, there were a lot of Jews there and a lot of Italian and other Jews had moved to Albania as a refuge, and they were all saved. Because the Albanians, as a population, thought it was even as their religion, they thought it was their duty to thwart the deportation of the Jews and they succeeded. So this is, as I tweeted as well, this is a counter-example to the narrative that some otherwise cogent commentators like you just did, assume. It's not necessarily the case. And it's also not necessarily the case that, so we've had in Britain...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: So, we've had Muslim immigrants and then their children are radicalized. Not them, their children are radicalized. Whose fault is that? That's not the fault of Islam. That's the fault of Britain and multiculturalism and pandering to the other and so on. And setting up the educational system so that it trashes Britain and glorifies the other and so on. But it needn't have been so. A hundred years ago, there were hundreds of thousands of Muslims in the British Army. Another 50 years earlier, there also were and they rebelled, but that's another story in the Indian Mutiny. But then so did the Hindus, so it again wasn't Islam, it was the fault of the West. Not for treating them too harshly, in a way for treating them too leniently, that they should have been integrated and it should have been understood that the same law applies to them as to everyone else. I'm a second-generation immigrant and I'm not violent and my parents came to this country and took for granted that they were going to have to work. And they took for granted that they were going to have to learn English and speak it well and get a job and so on and go to university and they took for granted all those things. And the idea that they were owed something by the surrounding culture was quite the opposite, that we owe something to the surrounding culture. That was their attitude.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: To what extent then is it an existential threat to the UK given you have had these huge protests recently taking over parts of London and I think you've had people under the auspices of the Green Party getting in and then coming out as vehemently anti-Semitic, anti...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: Yeah, no, that those people aren't the only people who are being indulged in this way. Basically, it's everyone who hates our culture is being indulged and everybody who is afraid of that is being demonized. The government can change that. I think the processes of criticism and persuasion are not impaired. The government isn't... like almost everything that Douglas Murray says about this issue is perfectly legal and is going to remain legal even under the most Draconian laws that are currently being proposed, which by the way may not succeed. There's a lot more to error correction in the British system than merely outvoting the government. There's going to be all sorts of because the government, one thing hasn't changed, the government no matter how large its majority knows that next week it could go down if suitable things happen. So the basic reason that the government is...

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: going down this wrong path is that most people think it's okay, I mean, most people don't think this is a disaster. They're like, well okay, we don't want riots, so let's have laws against riots. And and people are arguing but these aren't laws against riots. They're laws against hate or or speech and so on. But that that argument hasn't gone down with enough people, and when it does, it'll change. Oh yes, this is what David Starkey was saying. He was saying that the Red Wall, if you know what that is, that's...

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: the labor voting block.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Yeah, the labor voting block in Northern England which changed over to Boris Johnson en masse and changed back at this election. The Red Wall is never going to vote Labour again. Never ever. Because these measures are against it. The Labour Party has severed its connection with the working class.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And they got in by combination of circumstances because the Conservative Party was rubbish and and circumstances, the pandemic and all sorts of circumstances made people ready for a change. But they have gone down... now I don't think he's necessarily right that they'll never vote Labour again, because there are people in the Labour Party who can... who will be able to see what's happening long before the next election. And I'm sure that there are people in the Labour Party now who see that they can't go on like this. We'll see. Let's see if the current... the current woke legislation actually goes through when the Labour Party has a majority of 100 and something. That... that's not enough for it to work. Let's see. And if it goes through, the next level of error correction is that it'll never be implemented. Like, the Home Office people will not order the police chiefs to spend the money to implement the surveillance system which will be necessary to enforce the law. None of that will happen because on the whole, nothing happens. That's one of the things that's wrong with the blob... with the civil service. It prevents everything from happening. To have that happening, the government would have to pursue it. They would have to pursue it vigorously and ruthlessly, firing the people who didn't implement it and that kind of thing. And only then would... and then even then, when the first people go to jail for this kind of thing, there'll be an uproar. What are they going to do about it? Are they going to tell all the editors of the newspapers to say that this is proper and it should have happened? Yeah, at some point the editors of the newspapers are not going to automatically follow this line either. There will be a niche for...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: ...or people to contradict it. I-I don't think anything's inevitable.

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: And once that problem is solved, by the way, then the population problem will also be solved because the obvious way of solving the population problem is by immigration. Mass immigration is the way to go and we've only got to make sure that people who hate this country don't immigrate into it.

full_transcript.speaker: Brett Hall
full_transcript.text: Ah, but how do you...

full_transcript.speaker: David Deutsch
full_transcript.text: There'll be ways that... this is something that people who have thought a lot about this will think a lot about. At the moment they've been thinking how to do the opposite. If you, for example, you can have a rule that everybody who is allowed into the country has to declare that they... for a start, they could have to declare that they love the country and want to do well in it. And then if it later turns out they were lying, they can be—they can be deported, not for being enemies of Britain but for lying on the immigration form.

highlights: Fundamental ideas should be defined as those required for a broad range of explanations, meaning that emergent phenomena like life and thermodynamics are just as essential to understanding reality as microscopic physics.

Constructor theory seeks to redefine physics by focusing on which physical transformations are possible or impossible, providing a potential framework to unify the study of life, knowledge, and computation.

Human progress was stalled for hundreds of thousands of years by anti-rational memes, which are evolutionary-stabilized ideas designed to suppress creativity and individual guessing to keep cultures static and unchanging.

Knowledge is best understood as information that persists through error correction, a process that leads to "convergent evolution" where similar truths and structures emerge across different branches of the multiverse.

True learning is an active process of knowledge creation driven by an individual’s desire to solve their own problems, whereas traditional education often fails by treating the mind as a "bucket" to be filled with answers to unasked questions.
summary:
This transcript explores David Deutsch’s unified worldview, where the "four strands" of reality—quantum physics, computation, evolution, and epistemology—interconnect through the lens of knowledge and error correction. A central theme is the rejection of strict reductionism in favor of emergence; Deutsch argues that theories of life and mind are as fundamental as microscopic physics. Much of the technical discussion focuses on **Constructor Theory**, Deutsch’s framework that shifts the focus of physics from initial conditions and laws of motion to a description of which physical transformations are possible and which are impossible. This theory seeks to provide a more robust foundation for understanding information, thermodynamics, and the emergence of time.

The conversation also delves into the cultural and historical barriers to progress, specifically the role of **anti-rational memes**. Deutsch explains that for most of human history, creativity was directed toward preserving static cultures rather than innovation. He identifies modern educational institutions as primary propagators of anti-rationality, critiquing the "bucket theory of the mind" which treats students as passive recipients of data. Instead, he advocates for a view of learning as an active, learner-led process of knowledge creation, comparing the natural drive to learn to the way children master language or how Frederick Douglass risked his life to gain literacy.

Finally, the dialogue touches on the resilience of the Enlightenment and the modern Western political landscape. Despite concerns regarding "woke" culture, institutional stagnation, and political polarization, Deutsch remains a "Popperian" optimist. He argues that the unique strength of Western civilization lies in its capacity for error correction. While "fads" may temporarily degrade social institutions, he asserts that as long as the mechanisms of open criticism and persuasion remain intact, the Enlightenment is capable of overcoming current cultural burdens and continuing its trajectory of infinite progress.
